metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Strengthen AC2's proving command to assert that at least one metric value is non-null, e.g. add `and any(v.get('blocking_fp_proxy') is not None or v.get('advisory_application_rate') is not None for v in d.values())` so the all-null case fails.
evidence: Plan states: 'a non-trivial object with ≥1 criterion carrying all three of `blocking_fp_proxy`, `advisory_application_rate`, and a `sample_counts` block (NOT satisfiable by `{}`/all-null)'
Proving command uses `all(k in v for k in ('blocking_fp_proxy','advisory_application_rate','sample_counts'))` — `k in v` tests key presence, not value truthiness/non-null
A dict value of `None` satisfies `'blocking_fp_proxy' in {'blocking_fp_proxy': None}`
This is VCDT defect type 4 (fixture/assertion invalidity) and type 6 (assertion not on the structured content it claims to assert)
findings.finding: AC2's proving command checks only that the three metric *keys are present* in at least one criterion's dict, but it does NOT verify non-null/non-trivial values. An implementation that emits `{'some_criterion': {'blocking_fp_proxy': null, 'advisory_application_rate': null, 'sample_counts': {}}}` would satisfy `all(k in v for k in (...))` (key-presence check) yet the criterion explicitly states it is 'NOT satisfiable by `{}`/all-null'. The proving command therefore does not prove the non-triviality property it claims to enforce.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC2 proving command (the Python one-liner after `--emit &&`)
scenarios: An implementor emits all-None metric values (e.g. because no blocking findings exist in the corpus window) and the proving command exits 0, masking that the corpus join produced no data
CI passes on a trivially empty metrics object, giving false confidence that the instrumentation is working
findings.suggested_fix:
Replace the assertion in the proving command with:
```
assert d and any(
    all(k in v for k in ('blocking_fp_proxy','advisory_application_rate','sample_counts'))
    and (v.get('blocking_fp_proxy') is not None or v.get('advisory_application_rate') is not None)
    for v in d.values()
)
```
This ensures at least one criterion has both the required keys AND at least one non-null metric value, matching the 'NOT satisfiable by `{}`/all-null' claim.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the internal sub-fields and semantics of the `sample_counts` block in the per-criterion metrics schema (e.g., `{blocking_total, advisory_total, fp_proxy_numerator}`) so an implementer can emit a non-trivial, consistently shaped object.
evidence: AC2 proving command: `assert d and any(all(k in v for k in ('blocking_fp_proxy','advisory_application_rate','sample_counts')) for v in d.values())`

The What section defines `blocking_fp_proxy` and `advisory_application_rate` with precise formulas, but `sample_counts` is only named in the AC assertion — its sub-fields and structure are never specified in the plan.
ABSENCE: no definition of `sample_counts` sub-fields anywhere in the plan text
findings.finding: The `sample_counts` block is required in every per-criterion metric output by AC2's proving command (`assert ... 'sample_counts' in v`), but its internal schema — what sub-fields it contains and their semantics — is never defined anywhere in the plan. An implementer cannot determine whether `sample_counts` should hold raw integer counts of blocking/advisory/total findings, ticket counts, or something else.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria, AC2 — `--emit` proving command; 'Per-criterion trailing metrics' section in What
scenarios: Implementer emits `sample_counts: {}` or `sample_counts: 0` — both satisfy the AC key-presence check but provide no useful calibration data.
Two independent implementations produce incompatible `sample_counts` shapes, breaking downstream consumers that join on this field.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the 'Per-criterion trailing metrics' section: `sample_counts` = `{blocking_total: int, advisory_total: int, fp_proxy_numerator: int, advisory_adverse_numerator: int}` — the raw counts the two rate metrics are derived from.

findings.finding: The `sample_counts` block is required by the AC but its internal schema (sub-fields and their semantics) is never defined anywhere in the plan.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — `--emit` AC: '≥1 criterion carrying all three of `blocking_fp_proxy`, `advisory_application_rate`, and a `sample_counts` block'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the 'Per-criterion trailing metrics' section: '`sample_counts` is `{"n_blocking": <denominator of blocking_fp_proxy>, "n_advisory": <denominator of advisory_application_rate>, "n_fp_proxy_hits": <numerator of blocking_fp_proxy>, "window_rows": <actual N used>}`.

evidence: vague/subjective terms present: clean
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] In `gate_eval_instrumentation.py`, import `RECALL_MIN_PRIORITY` from `rebar.llm.plan_review.sidecar` (the module already used for `all_review_results`) rather than hardcoding `0.5` in the `has_strong_finding` predicate, and note this import in the Scope section.
evidence: Plan: 'The 0.5 floor is the codebase's own RECALL_MIN_PRIORITY (src/rebar/llm/plan_review/sidecar.py:299)'
Plan specifies that the harness already imports from rebar.llm.plan_review.sidecar via all_review_results — so the module is in scope for import
Hardcoding 0.5 instead of importing creates silent coupling drift if the constant is tuned
findings.finding: The `has_strong_finding` predicate hardcodes `priority >= 0.5` rather than importing the `RECALL_MIN_PRIORITY` constant from `src/rebar/llm/plan_review/sidecar.py:299`. If that constant evolves, the harness will silently diverge from production semantics, undermining the monitoring tool's alignment with the gate it is measuring.
scenarios: RECALL_MIN_PRIORITY is raised to 0.6 during a future calibration; the harness still uses 0.5, classifying fewer findings as 'strong' and mislabeling outcomes relative to what the live gate would see.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace `priority >= 0.5` with `from rebar.llm.plan_review.sidecar import RECALL_MIN_PRIORITY; ... priority >= RECALL_MIN_PRIORITY` in the has_strong_finding extractor. Update the plan's Scope bullet to call out this import explicitly.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that the harness checks the subprocess return code and, on non-zero exit, either raises a clear error (printing miner stderr) or — if `--no-refresh` semantics are desired as fallback — documents that behaviour explicitly in the plan.
evidence: 'The job refreshes the outcome corpus by invoking mine_outcome_corpus.py as a subprocess ... then reads that JSONL'

'that entry calls sys.exit() on floor-check failure (mine_outcome_corpus.py:381), which would terminate the caller' — acknowledged as the reason for subprocess isolation, but no recovery path specified for the caller when the subprocess itself exits non-zero
No mention of checking subprocess return code, logging failure, or falling back to the committed corpus on miner failure
findings.finding: The plan specifies that the harness invokes mine_outcome_corpus.py as a subprocess but does not specify error handling when that subprocess fails (e.g., when the miner's floor-check calls sys.exit(1), or when the miner crashes due to missing git history or network issues). The harness would either crash uncleanly or silently proceed to read a stale/absent corpus.
findings.location: What section — 'Corpus acquisition (subprocess, not import)' paragraph
scenarios: Miner floor-check fails (not enough rows) → subprocess exits non-zero → harness reads stale or absent JSONL → metrics are silently wrong or harness crashes with an unhelpful FileNotFoundError
CI environment lacks git history needed by miner → subprocess fails → --emit produces no output → CI assertion fails with no diagnostic
findings.suggested_fix: Add: 'If the subprocess exits non-zero, the harness prints the miner's stderr and exits non-zero itself (no silent fallback to a stale corpus). Operators who want offline behaviour must pass --no-refresh explicitly.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that the --emit write uses an atomic write pattern (write to a .tmp file, then os.replace() to the final path) so a partial write never leaves a corrupt JSON artifact.
evidence: '--emit writes per-criterion metrics to docs/experiments/plan-review-gate/runs/gate_eval_metrics.json'
No mention of temp-file+rename or any write-safety mechanism
The CI proving command does json.load() directly on the file — a partial write would cause an undiagnosed parse error
findings.finding: The plan specifies that --emit writes gate_eval_metrics.json but does not require an atomic write (write-to-temp then rename). A crash or keyboard interrupt mid-write leaves a truncated/corrupt JSON file that subsequent reads (including the CI proving command) will fail on with a parse error rather than a clean 'file absent' signal.
findings.location: Scope section — gate_eval_instrumentation.py entry; AC line 2 (--emit AC)
scenarios: Operator hits Ctrl-C during a slow --emit run → gate_eval_metrics.json is half-written → next CI run fails with JSONDecodeError, not a useful diagnostic
findings.suggested_fix: Add to scope: 'The --emit write uses os.replace(tmp_path, final_path) after writing to a sibling .tmp file, ensuring the output is never partially visible.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify and test the subprocess failure path: define the expected behavior when mine_outcome_corpus.py exits non-zero (e.g., re-raise with a human-readable message, exit non-zero), add a corresponding AC or unit-test assertion (e.g., assert the harness exits non-zero and prints a diagnostic when the subprocess fails), and handle the case where the output JSONL is absent or empty.
evidence: 'The job refreshes the outcome corpus by invoking mine_outcome_corpus.py as a subprocess … which atomically writes runs/outcome_corpus.jsonl, then reads that JSONL.'
No AC or test specification addresses what happens when the subprocess exits non-zero, the output file is missing, or the JSONL is malformed/empty.
The --no-refresh flag is an alternative but does not cover subprocess failure behavior in the default (refresh) path.
findings.finding: The plan specifies subprocess invocation of mine_outcome_corpus.py as the corpus-refresh path but does not specify error-handling behavior (e.g., non-zero exit, missing file, malformed JSONL output), and no AC or test covers this failure path — the harness could fail silently or raise an unhandled exception in normal operator use.
findings.location: What section — 'Corpus acquisition (subprocess, not import)'; AC list (no subprocess-failure AC present)
scenarios: mine_outcome_corpus.py fails due to missing sidecar files or floor-check failure → gate_eval_instrumentation.py swallows the error and reads stale/absent corpus, emitting misleading metrics.
Operator runs --emit in CI without --no-refresh; subprocess exits non-zero; harness crashes with an unhandled CalledProcessError and the CI job gives no actionable error message.
findings.suggested_fix: Add an AC: 'When mine_outcome_corpus.py exits non-zero, gate_eval_instrumentation.py exits non-zero with a human-readable error (not a bare traceback); proving command: `python .../gate_eval_instrumentation.py 2>&1 | grep -i "corpus refresh failed"` after forcing subprocess failure.' Add a corresponding unit test that mocks subprocess.run to return non-zero and asserts SystemExit or a logged error.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Consider extracting the shared `_atomic_write` pattern into a small `harness_utils.py` in `harnesses/` (or at minimum confirm in the plan whether `gate_eval_instrumentation.py` will reuse an existing copy vs. duplicate it a third time), since `mine_outcome_corpus.py:389` and `build_adjudication_corpus.py:190` already contain identical implementations.
evidence: harnesses/mine_outcome_corpus.py:389: `def _atomic_write_jsonl(rows, path)` — tempfile.mkstemp + os.replace pattern
harnesses/build_adjudication_corpus.py:190: identical `def _atomic_write_jsonl` copy
Plan Scope section specifies `--emit` writes to `runs/gate_eval_metrics.json` but does not specify whether it reuses or re-copies the atomic write pattern
Rule-of-Three: 3 independent copies of the same utility function in the same harnesses/ directory
findings.finding: The new `gate_eval_instrumentation.py` harness will need to write `gate_eval_metrics.json` atomically (the `--emit` path), yet the identical `_atomic_write_jsonl` helper already exists as independent copies in `mine_outcome_corpus.py:389` and `build_adjudication_corpus.py:190`. Adding the new script's write logic makes this a third copy of the same pattern, crossing the Rule-of-Three threshold for premature duplication within the harness directory.
scenarios: A third independent copy of the atomic-write helper drifts (e.g., one copy fixes a tmp-cleanup edge case) while the others don't, making behavior inconsistent across harness outputs.
A reviewer or future maintainer adding a fourth harness copy-pastes a stale version of the pattern.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a one-function `harnesses/harness_utils.py` exporting `atomic_write_json(obj, path)` (a JSON variant) and `atomic_write_jsonl(rows, path)`, then import it in all three harnesses. This is a docs/experiments/-internal refactor with no src/rebar/ impact.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a note to the `docs/plan-review-gate.md` "Blocking-FP-proxy demotion" section warning that a `criteria_routing.json` `default_posture` edit must also keep `CANONICAL_LLM` in parity (the CI gate at `test.yml:336-344` enforces this).
evidence: Plan: 'demote it from blocking to advisory via an ordinary `criteria_routing.json` edit (`default_posture: "advisory"`)' — no mention of CANONICAL_LLM parity requirement
.github/workflows/test.yml:336-344: CI parity gate checks criteria_routing.json against CANONICAL_LLM
tests/unit/test_criteria_overlay.py:305: 'The committed criteria_routing.json is in parity with CANONICAL_LLM + structurally…'
search result: 'echo "::error::src/rebar/llm/plan_review/criteria_routing.json is out of parity"'
findings.finding: The demotion playbook section being added to docs/plan-review-gate.md instructs operators to edit `src/rebar/llm/plan_review/criteria_routing.json` (set `default_posture: "advisory"`), but does not enumerate the CI parity gate that guards that file: a `criteria_routing.json` edit must stay in parity with `CANONICAL_LLM` (enforced by `test.yml:336-344` and pinned by `tests/unit/test_criteria_overlay.py:305`). An operator following the playbook verbatim could break CI if they only change `default_posture` without updating CANONICAL_LLM.
findings.location: Scope / Alarm & demotion rule section — 'demote it from blocking to advisory via an ordinary `criteria_routing.json` edit (`default_posture: "advisory"`)'
scenarios: Operator follows the playbook to demote a criterion, edits criteria_routing.json's default_posture from 'blocking' to 'advisory', but does not know to also update CANONICAL_LLM — CI gate fails.
findings.suggested_fix: In the demotion playbook section, add: 'Note: `criteria_routing.json` is parity-gated against `CANONICAL_LLM` in CI — update both in the same commit, or the parity test (`test_criteria_overlay.py`) will fail.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify that `--window N` selects the last N rows by **alphabetical ticket-id sort** (random w.r.t. time), not by recency — either rename the behavior (e.g. `--sample-tail N`) or add a `--most-recent` flag that filters by `review_round_count`-max timestamp from the git-walk, and document the chosen semantic in the README and AC.
evidence: Plan states: 'emission order is the stable proxy, documented as such — a strictly chronological window is future work needing a timestamp'
mine_outcome_corpus.py:379: `reviewed = sorted(tid for tid, tk in store.items() if review_round_count(tk) > 0)` — sorts by ticket_id string, which is random UUID hex
Confirmed corpus ordering: rows 1-5 start with '003d-...', '0128-...'; rows 620-628 end with 'fd84-...', 'fde0-...', 'ff6f-...', 'ff7d-...' — lexicographic, not chronological
findings.finding: The `--window N` mechanism uses 'emission order' as a chronological proxy, but the miner sorts the corpus by `sorted(tid ...)` — alphabetical UUID hex — which is random w.r.t. ticket creation/review time. `--window 200` therefore selects the 200 lexicographically-last IDs (those starting with 'f...'), not the 200 most recently reviewed tickets. Because UUID hex prefixes are assigned randomly, new tickets are uniformly distributed across the alphabet, so the 'last N by sort order' window does not drift toward recent work as new tickets land. This defeats the stated purpose of detecting *recent* criterion FP drift.
findings.location: What section — `--window N` definition; Scope — `gate_eval_instrumentation.py` CLI flags
scenarios: A criterion starts generating FPs on tickets created in the last two months. `--window 200` selects tickets alphabetically ending in 'f...', which were created randomly across the full ticket lifespan — diluting the recent FP signal and potentially missing the drift entirely.
After a gate calibration change, `--window 50` is used to check recent behavior. It selects the ~50 tickets with IDs starting with 'fe'-'ff', again a random temporal sample.
findings.suggested_fix: The lowest-cost fix: rename `--window N` to `--tail N` and document its alphabetical-sort semantics clearly (not as a temporal proxy). The higher-fidelity fix: since the outcome corpus has no timestamp, expose a `--since-ticket <id>` flag or add a `first_review_ts` field to the corpus (one additional git-walk column), then filter on it. Given the plan already flags this as 'future work needing a timestamp,' the minimum acceptable action is to remove the 'stable proxy' framing and document the actual behavior so the playbook's monitoring cadence advice is accurate.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Align the leaf with the parent's event-driven write-back contract: either add the 'append a gate-eval record (sidecar-style, reducer-ignored) on every material post-claim edit' path, or update the parent first to ratify the batch-only architecture (which forces parent re-review before this leaf can be re-reviewed).
evidence: Parent: 'on every material post-claim description edit, link the edit to the ticket's latest REVIEW_RESULT and append a gate-eval record (sidecar-style, reducer-ignored) classifying it MISSED / CAUGHT-BUT-IGNORED / UNKNOWABLE'
Leaf Scope: 'docs/experiments/plan-review-gate/harnesses/gate_eval_instrumentation.py — the job … CLI flags --verify-repro, --emit, --window N, --no-refresh'
Leaf ACs describe --emit writing to `runs/gate_eval_metrics.json`; no AC mentions appending events to any ticket store
ABSENCE: no leaf section describes a write path to the ticket event store
findings.finding: The leaf drops the parent's event-driven write-back architecture. The parent says 'on every material post-claim description edit … append a gate-eval record (sidecar-style, reducer-ignored)' — a per-event write into the ticket store. The leaf delivers a batch, read-only harness that appends nothing to any ticket; it writes only an aggregate `runs/gate_eval_metrics.json`. This is not a consistent narrowing; it substitutes a different architecture without updating the parent.
scenarios: Post-claim edits on new tickets accumulate with no gate-eval record emitted; downstream R1–R6 experiments that join on per-ticket gate-eval records find none, defeating the parent's 'durable measurement substrate' goal.
The offline batch job requires manual re-runs to see new data, whereas the parent's design auto-captures each edit as it happens.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Update the parent (§R7 of `task-decomposition-sota-2026.md`) to ratify the deterministic cascade classifier before landing the leaf — the parent currently specifies 'initially by LLM with human spot-audit', which the leaf contradicts; the parent wins on conflict until it is explicitly updated.
evidence: Parent: 'classifying it MISSED / CAUGHT-BUT-IGNORED / UNKNOWABLE — initially by LLM with human spot-audit, using §5.2's rubric'

Leaf 'What': 'Outcome classifier (deterministic; the honest finding is that the sidecar findings do NOT carry the separating signal)' — a deterministic C1–C4 cascade over `post_claim_edit_class` and `review_round_count`
The leaf's README is directed to document the 4/8-vs-7/8 finding (the methodological rationale for the switch), but the parent has not been updated to reflect this change
findings.finding: The leaf redefines the classification mechanism from LLM-based (with human spot-audit) to a deterministic rule cascade without updating the parent. The parent specifies 'initially by LLM with human spot-audit, using §5.2's rubric'; the leaf delivers a deterministic first-match C1–C4 cascade over outcome-corpus fields. Under the G7 conflict rule the parent wins; a silent change to the method without updating the parent is a divergence.
findings.location: parent §R7 'What' ('initially by LLM with human spot-audit') vs leaf 'What' ('Outcome classifier (deterministic)')
scenarios: A reviewer comparing the delivered harness to the parent spec finds a fundamental methodology mismatch — LLM-classified records vs deterministic-rule-classified records — with no parent update to ratify the change.
If future work re-runs the parent's intended LLM classifier path, it produces a different corpus than the leaf's deterministic one, creating two incompatible labeling schemes.
findings.suggested_fix: Update §R7's 'What' in the parent to read something like: 'classifying it MISSED / CAUGHT-BUT-IGNORED / UNKNOWABLE — via a deterministic rule cascade over outcome-corpus fields + a sidecar strength predicate (LLM-based classification was investigated but achieves only 4/8; the deterministic cascade achieves 7/8 — see the harness README).' Then re-review the parent before re-reviewing this leaf.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Acknowledge the two existing identical `_atomic_write_jsonl` helpers in sibling harness scripts (`mine_outcome_corpus.py:389`, `build_adjudication_corpus.py:190`) and specify whether the new harness inlines the same pattern or extracts a shared utility to avoid introducing a third copy.
evidence: mine_outcome_corpus.py:389 defines `def _atomic_write_jsonl(rows, path)` using tempfile.mkstemp + os.replace
build_adjudication_corpus.py:190 defines an identical `def _atomic_write_jsonl(rows, path)` — same body
The plan's `--emit` path must write `gate_eval_metrics.json` atomically but the plan is silent on reuse vs. duplication of this helper
ABSENCE: plan does not mention or cite either existing helper as prior art or a sharing candidate
findings.finding: The new `gate_eval_instrumentation.py` harness will need atomic JSON writing for its `--emit` path, yet an identical `_atomic_write_jsonl` private helper (tempfile + os.replace) already exists in two sibling scripts in the same directory (`mine_outcome_corpus.py:389` and `build_adjudication_corpus.py:190`). The plan does not acknowledge this prior art and would introduce a third copy of the same utility.
scenarios: Implementer writes a third copy of the helper, deepening the DRY violation across all three harness scripts in the same directory
A future bug fix to the atomic-write pattern must be applied three times instead of once
findings.suggested_fix: Add a note in the Scope section: 'The `--emit` write uses the same `_atomic_write_jsonl` pattern as the sibling harnesses (tempfile + os.replace); either inline it or extract a `_harness_utils.py` helper shared by all three scripts.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Co-locate with the `has_strong_finding` predicate definition: the source of the `severity` field (it is the output of `severity_label(impact)` in `review_kernel/decide.py`), the full closed enum `{"critical","major","minor","none"}`, and the impact thresholds that produce each value.
evidence: Plan text: '`has_strong_finding` ... ∃ a surfaced sidecar finding with `decision ∈ {"block","advisory"}` AND `priority >= 0.5` AND `severity ∈ {"critical","major"}`'
`severity_label()` in `src/rebar/llm/review_kernel/decide.py:299`: returns 'critical' (imp>=0.75), 'major' (imp>=0.5), 'minor' (imp>=0.25), 'none' (<0.25)
Sidecar persists `severity` at `src/rebar/llm/plan_review/sidecar.py:430` as `f.get('severity')` — a string derived from `severity_label(impact)`
ABSENCE: plan does not cite `severity_label()` or explain the float→string derivation or the full 4-value enum
findings.finding: The `has_strong_finding` predicate requires `severity ∈ {"critical","major"}` but the plan never defines where the `severity` string originates in a sidecar finding, what the complete severity enum is, or how the mapping from `impact` float to severity label works. The full enum `{"critical","major","minor","none"}` and its derivation from `severity_label(impact)` in `review_kernel/decide.py` (thresholds: ≥0.75→critical, ≥0.5→major) are only discoverable by reading the codebase, not the plan.
scenarios: An implementer reads `severity` as an LLM-emitted free-text field and applies a different set of values, causing `has_strong_finding` to silently return False for all findings.
An implementer excludes 'minor' but not 'none' (or vice versa) due to not knowing the full vocabulary, producing a subtly wrong predicate that misclassifies UNKNOWABLE cases.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a parenthetical to the `has_strong_finding` definition: '(`severity` is the `severity_label(impact)` string from `review_kernel/decide.py:299`; full enum: `{"critical","major","minor","none"}`; the conjunct `severity ∈ {"critical","major"}` corresponds to `impact >= 0.5`, i.e. the same RECALL_MIN_PRIORITY floor applied to the impact dimension).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the error-handling policy for a non-zero subprocess exit in the corpus-acquisition step: either (a) re-raise with a clear error message and non-zero exit, or (b) automatically fall back to `--no-refresh` behavior with a logged warning.
evidence: Plan text: 'The job refreshes the outcome corpus by invoking `mine_outcome_corpus.py` as a subprocess ... A `--no-refresh` flag skips regeneration and reads the committed corpus (offline / CI use).'
Plan text: 'which atomically writes `runs/outcome_corpus.jsonl`'
ABSENCE: no specified error-handling path when subprocess returns non-zero exit code
`mine_outcome_corpus.py:381`: the miner calls `sys.exit()` on floor-check failure — the exact scenario the plan uses as motivation for subprocess isolation, but provides no caller-side recovery
findings.finding: No fallback is specified for the case where the subprocess invocation of `mine_outcome_corpus.py` exits non-zero (e.g., floor-check failure, missing git objects, no network). The plan explains WHY subprocess is used (to avoid `sys.exit()` propagation) but does not specify what `gate_eval_instrumentation.py` should do on a non-zero exit code: re-raise, print an error and exit with a specific code, or degrade to `--no-refresh` mode.
findings.location: What section — 'Corpus acquisition (subprocess, not import)' paragraph; Scope — `gate_eval_instrumentation.py`
scenarios: Subprocess fails with exit code 1 (floor check: <500 reviewed tickets recovered from git); harness silently continues reading a stale or missing `outcome_corpus.jsonl`, computing metrics over a truncated corpus with no warning.
Subprocess fails in CI where git objects are unavailable; harness crashes with an unhandled `CalledProcessError` rather than degrading to `--no-refresh`.

findings.finding: The AC4 proving command `grep -q 'T5e' docs/plan-review-gate.md` already passes on the current file (T5e is mentioned at line 142 in the calibration table) without any edit from this ticket — so the command cannot verify that the *new* alarm/demotion playbook content citing T5e demotion precedent was actually added.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace the T5e grep with a phrase that only appears in the new content, e.g.: `grep -q 'blocking_fp_proxy' docs/plan-review-gate.md && grep -q 'T5e' docs/plan-review-gate.md`

findings.finding: The AC4 proving command uses `grep -q "T5e" docs/plan-review-gate.md` to verify the new demotion-precedent content, but the plan itself acknowledges 'the incidental T5e mention already in the calibration table' — meaning T5e already appears in that file before any changes. The grep will always pass (pre-existing match), so it does NOT verify that the new alarm-rule T5e demotion-precedent text was actually added.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace `grep -q "T5e" docs/plan-review-gate.md` with `grep -qE 'T5e.*(demot|precedent)|demotion.*T5e' docs/plan-review-gate.md` — this only matches when T5e appears alongside demotion language, which is the new content, not the old calibration-table row.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add to the README and/or `docs/plan-review-gate.md` playbook: (a) a note that `advisory_application_rate` is confounded by co-occurring blocking findings on the same ticket (the pre-claim remediation loop drives most post-claim edits), so it should be read alongside blocking co-occurrence counts; and (b) a stated interpretation baseline or explicit 'observational only — no alarm threshold' declaration, parallel to the >10% rule for blocking_fp_proxy.
evidence: Plan defines: 'advisory_application_rate(C) = (# of C's advisory findings whose ticket later shows an adverse post-claim edit) / (total # of C's advisory findings)' — no alarm threshold stated
Contrast: blocking_fp_proxy has '> 10% alarm triggers human review of that criterion's recent blocks'
Plan notes: 'blocking findings are remediated pre-claim in the review loop' — the same logic applies in reverse for advisories on tickets that were blocked
findings.finding: The `advisory_application_rate(C)` metric is defined but has no stated alarm threshold, baseline, or interpretation guide — unlike `blocking_fp_proxy` which has a clear >10% action rule. Additionally, for tickets that had BOTH a blocking finding (from any criterion) AND an advisory finding from criterion C, post-claim edits are primarily driven by the block resolution loop (pre-claim), not by the advisory — yet the metric counts any adverse post-claim edit as 'advisory applied,' conflating the two without flagging the confound.
findings.location: What section — per-criterion trailing metrics definition; `advisory_application_rate(C)` definition
scenarios: A criterion has advisory_application_rate of 40% because 40% of its advisory-finding tickets also had blocking findings from other criteria that forced pre-claim plan revisions — the rate looks high but the advisory finding itself may have been ignored.

An operator sees advisory_application_rate = 5% vs 35% for two criteria and wants to know which is better-calibrated — but without a baseline or action threshold, the metric is uninterpretable for monitoring purposes.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
